abundance.title: Issue 18 | Works in Progress
abundance.reason: Red de vapor centenaria de Manhattan
abundance.snippet: Works in Progress Website New York's skyscrapers soar above a century-old steam network that still warms the city. While the rest of the world moved to hot water, Manhattanites still buy steam by the megapound.

abundance.title: Immigration Policy Solutions to Shortages in Critical Sectors of ... - CSIS
abundance.snippet: Despite the influx of proposals and debate, optimism forimmigrationreformremains unlikely. Rather than address the details ofimmigrationlawreform, this paper explores the current state of the U.S. economy and the role migrants can play. The U.S.labormarket cooled in the first half of 2024, but critical sectors remain badly understaffed.

abundance.title: German housing crisis: government plans construction boost
abundance.snippet: Germanyhas a desperate shortage of affordablehousing. The government now plans to take a "crowbar" toconstructionlaw to help get more homes built at "turbo" speed.

articles.title: What can the world’s most walkable cities teach other places?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Some cities, like Copenhagen, Paris, and Tokyo, are designed to be very walkable, with everything residents need close by. These "15-minute cities" allow people to access shops, schools, and parks easily on foot. A recent study highlights how other urban areas can learn from these examples to improve their walkability.
articles.reason: Ciudades caminables como modelo de abundancia urbana

articles.title: It’s Electricity Realism, Not Climate Denialism
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: The global energy system is undergoing a major change due to the electrification of everything. However, this transition faces challenges from cultural debates and the need to address risks associated with electric technologies. As electricity demand continues to rise, it’s crucial for advocates of clean energy solutions to confront these issues.
articles.reason: Realismo eléctrico ante el reto energético

articles.title: PG&E Project Aims to Ease AI Computing Need and Housing Shortage
articles.author: Mark Chediak
articles.summary: PG&E is partnering with Westbank to build a zero-carbon data center and housing project in downtown San Jose. The plan includes three data centers and up to 4,000 new homes. Excess heat from the data centers will be used to heat nearby buildings.

articles.title: Lo que nos dicen las revisiones del INE: las empresas han ganado más y los salarios, menos
articles.author: Javier Jorrín
articles.summary: La revisión de las series muestra un comportamiento más dinámico de los beneficios y más pobre de los salarios. Se corrigen discrepancias con otras fuentes estadísticas

articles.title: La brecha ideológica de los jóvenes se agranda: ellos, cada vez más de derechas y ellas, más de izquierdas
articles.author: Javier Jorrín
articles.summary: Por primera vez en la época reciente surge una diferenciación ideológica grande dentro de una generación. Los hombres jóvenes viven una realidad hostil que genera resentimiento
articles.reason: Polarización generacional por género en política

articles.title: Raising employment among women and older workers: A policy lever for ageing economies
articles.author: VoxEU | CEPR
articles.summary: Aging populations and low birth rates are causing fewer people to work, which hurts the economy. Increasing jobs for women and older workers can help fix this problem. Policies like better childcare, flexible work, and later retirement can boost employment and support growth.
articles.reason: Empleo femenino como solución al envejecimiento

articles.title: China’s Coal Industry Has a Big, Dirty Secret
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: China's coal industry has a massive and often overlooked sector that converts coal into chemical products and liquid fuels, consuming about 380 million metric tons of coal. This industry is expanding rapidly, despite China’s efforts to transition to green energy, and poses significant challenges for global climate goals. The reliance on coal for energy security and economic stability means that coal demand in China is likely to remain high for the foreseeable future.

articles.title: How politics shapes the world’s time zones
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Politics heavily influences time zones around the world, often leading to significant discrepancies between neighboring regions. Countries may adjust their time zones for national identity, economic reasons, or political statements. Examples include China's unified "Beijing time" and Spain's continued use of Central European Time due to historical decisions.
articles.reason: Política como obstáculo a la eficiencia temporal

articles.title: Our Carrie Bradshaw index shows Australia’s housing is in crisis
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Australia is facing a housing crisis as many struggle to afford homes. House prices have doubled compared to incomes over the last 25 years. The rental market is overwhelmed with demand and has a lack of supply.
articles.reason: Crisis habitacional medida con datos originales

articles.title: Where can Americans afford to live solo in 2025?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: The cost of rent varies a lot across American cities, more than wages do. Many southern cities are becoming too expensive for people who want to live alone. The “Carrie Bradshaw index” ranks 100 cities by how affordable they are for solo renters.

articles.title: Bruselas avisa: España es el país que más recaudación tendrá que destinar a las pensiones
articles.author: Javier Jorrín
articles.summary: La generosidad del sistema español y el rápido envejecimiento que va a vivir el país llevará a destinar el 46% de los ingresos fiscales (incluyendo cotizaciones) a pagar las pensiones
articles.reason: Insostenibilidad fiscal del sistema pensiones español

articles.title: AI Boom Is Driving a Surprise Resurgence of US Gas-Fired Power
articles.author: Josh Saul
articles.summary: US energy companies are rapidly building new natural gas power plants to meet rising demand. This demand is driven by AI data centers, manufacturing, and electric vehicles. Experts are surprised by the continued growth of gas as a major energy source in the country.

articles.title: How “bloated” are governments really?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Donald Trump and Elon Musk claim that the federal government is too large and wasteful, leading to mass layoffs of public-sector workers. Musk's Department of Government Efficiency has already cut 56,000 jobs, with more layoffs expected. Other countries, like Argentina and Britain, are also reducing government staff to improve efficiency.

articles.title: Can AI replace junior workers?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: America's job growth has slowed sharply from April to August. A study of many companies hints that generative AI may be reducing the need for junior workers. The full impact of AI on jobs is still unclear in official data.
articles.reason: IA transformando estructura del mercado laboral

articles.title: Así murió la democracia alemana de Weimar (¿podría morir igual la nuestra?)
articles.author: Ramón González Férriz
articles.summary: Un nuevo y brillante libro reconstruye los catorce años de la república alemana de entreguerras y cuenta cómo las malas decisiones provocaron su final de la mano de Hitler
articles.reason: Lecciones democráticas de Weimar para hoy

articles.title: Can AI be trusted in schools?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: AI tools like ChatGPT are causing issues in schools by making students overly reliant on them for assignments. In some countries, when these tools were removed, students struggled more than those who didn't use them. Additionally, some teachers are using AI to provide generic feedback, leading to complaints from students.
articles.reason: IA en educación requiere regulación inteligente

articles.title: GE Vernova to Power City-Sized Data Centers With Gas as AI Demand Soars
articles.author: Naureen S Malik
articles.summary: GE Vernova has signed contracts to provide natural gas turbines for large data centers that will use enough energy to power major cities. These turbines are being reserved by big tech companies for their upcoming 5-gigawatt data center projects. The companies plan to have some of these facilities operational by 2028.

articles.title: Who is ahead in the global tech race?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: A new index from Harvard ranks 25 countries based on their technological strength in areas like AI, semiconductors, and biotechnology. The United States leads the rankings, but other nations are catching up. This competition impacts economic growth, military power, and global influence.

articles.title: Ahora o nunca: Europa necesita ambición para recuperar el crecimiento económico
articles.author: J. Jorrín
articles.summary: El escenario económico de Europa era malo antes de Trump, y ahora es peor. Pero la UE tiene una oportunidad para reaccionar y realizar las reformas estructurales que funcionan

articles.title: AI Boom Has Generac Looking to Data Centers for Growth
articles.author: Mark Chediak
articles.summary: Generac Holdings Inc. is expanding from home backup generators to focus on data centers. The company is investing $130 million to produce larger generators for AI applications. They have started shipping these new generators to international data center customers.

articles.title: Xcel CEO Says AI Power Needs Raise Prospect of New Large Nuclear
articles.author: Josh Saul
articles.summary: Bob Frenzel Photographer: Kent Nishimura/Bloomberg
articles.reason: Nuclear como respuesta escalable a IA

articles.title: Data Centers Will Have to Make Minimum Power Payments in Ohio
articles.author: Mark Chediak
articles.summary: Data centers in Ohio will now be required to pay at least 85% of their energy costs to support grid improvements. This agreement was made by American Electric Power Co. and various stakeholders, including the state regulator and Walmart. The settlement aims to ensure that energy infrastructure can meet growing demands.

articles.title: AI to Prop Up Fossil Fuels and Slow Emissions Decline, BNEF Says
articles.author: Shoko Oda
articles.summary: A new report from BNEF suggests that the growth of AI will hinder global efforts to reduce emissions in the next decade. The rise of AI technology is expected to support fossil fuel usage. This could slow down the decline in carbon emissions.

articles.title: Blackstone Makes $5.7 Billion Bet on Power With Deal to Buy TXNM
articles.author: Mark Chediak
articles.summary: Blackstone Infrastructure is set to buy TXNM Energy Inc. for $5.7 billion. TXNM, which serves over 800,000 homes in New Mexico and Texas, was previously trying to be acquired by another company but that deal fell through. The acquisition reflects a total value of $11.5 billion, including debt.

articles.title: US Regulator Rejects Amazon-Talen Nuclear Power Agreement
articles.author: Naureen S Malik
articles.summary: The Federal Energy Regulatory Commission rejected an agreement that would have allowed an Amazon Inc.com data center to directly tap into power from an Pennsylvania nuclear plant.

articles.title: Did international trade really kill American manufacturing?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: President Trump claims that international trade has hurt American manufacturing and aims to bring jobs back to the U.S. through tariffs. However, data shows that the decline in manufacturing jobs is part of larger economic trends, not just trade policies. In 1950, nearly one in three workers were in manufacturing, but today it's only one in twelve.

articles.title: España es el gran país europeo donde los funcionarios más cobran frente al sector privado
articles.author: Javier Jorrín
articles.summary: La brecha salarial alcanza el 9%, sólo superada por Chipre y Luxemburgo. En España, los gobiernos emplean las retribuciones públicas como herramienta electoral

articles.title: Demand for American degrees has already hit covid-era lows
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Demand for American degrees has dropped to levels seen during the COVID-19 pandemic. Many successful American companies were started by immigrants who came to study in the U.S. A significant number of these startups were founded by individuals who first arrived as students.

articles.title: Lessons From the First Blackout of the Green Era
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: Spain and Portugal experienced a major blackout, affecting over 50 million people, due to issues with renewable energy generation. The power failure was likely caused by a solar plant underperforming, leading to a significant drop in electricity from other renewable sources. Authorities are now analyzing the incident to improve the integration of renewable energy into the grid for future stability.

articles.title: Which countries would benefit most from an American brain drain?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: The U.S. is becoming less welcoming to talented immigrants due to recent policy changes. This has led other countries to strategize on attracting American graduates and professionals. An analysis identifies which nations could benefit most from this potential brain drain.

articles.title: Madrid es la CCAA que más peso gana en el PIB nacional, Cataluña y Euskadi, las que pierden
articles.author: Javier Jorrín
articles.summary: El crecimiento económico sigue girando en torno a la capital, que supone casi el 20% del total nacional. Las CCAA despobladas sostienen su PIB per cápita por las pensiones
articles.reason: Centralización económica en Madrid vs regiones

articles.title: Which countries breed Nobel laureates, and which import them?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: This year, six out of nine Nobel prize winners in science worked in the United States. Many of these top researchers were born in other countries. Science is becoming more global, with more immigrants making big discoveries.

articles.title: Self-driving cars will transform urban economies
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: Self-driving robotaxis are starting to change how people travel in cities. These cars work differently from regular cars and will reshape urban areas. This change will be bigger than anything since cars were first invented.

articles.title: ¿Se acabó apretarse el cinturón? Hogares y empresas se endeudan 15 años después
articles.author: Javier Jorrín
articles.summary: El pasivo del sector privado ha vuelto a crecer tras un largo ajuste. El crecimiento económico, la bajada de los tipos de interés y la revalorización de los activos están borrando las preocupaciones del pasado
articles.reason: Fin del desapalancamiento tras crisis financiera

articles.title: The world’s most innovative countries
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Germany is known for many inventions but is no longer in the top ten most innovative countries. China has taken Germany's place in the global innovation ranking. The ranking is based on patent applications filed by 139 countries.

articles.title: DeepSeek’s AI Model Just Upended the White-Hot US Power Market
articles.author: Michelle Ma
articles.summary: DeepSeek has developed an AI model that has significantly impacted the US power market. This new technology is changing how energy is analyzed and traded. The shift could lead to major changes in the industry's operations.

articles.title: The McWages index: which countries earn the most Big Macs?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: The McWages index compares how many Big Macs workers can buy with their salaries in different countries. American workers can buy more than six times as many Big Macs as Mexican workers. This shows where pay goes further, using the Big Mac as a simple way to measure affordability.

articles.title: Distintas recetas, mismo final: la Argentina de Milei se asoma al abismo financiero
articles.author: Javier Jorrín
articles.summary: La obsesión del Gobierno por mantener sobrevalorado el peso está provocando el vaciamiento de reservas del banco central. EEUU planea intervenir para frenar la tormenta

articles.title: What is the richest country in the world in 2025?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: The richest country in 2025 is measured in three ways, not just by income. Prices and work hours affect how wealthy people feel in each place. This helps show which countries are truly rich.
articles.reason: Riqueza nacional más allá del PIB

articles.title: America’s huge mortgage market is slowly dying
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: America’s mortgage market, once the heart of the American dream, is shrinking. Government support for home loans is fading. This decline may worsen the housing crisis.

articles.title: Donald Trump’s tariffs defy geopolitical logic
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: President Donald Trump announced new tariffs that contradict geopolitical logic, impacting both allies and adversaries. He plans to impose a 10% duty on China and a 25% duty on Mexico and Canada, although the latter tariffs will be paused for a month. These measures aim to address trade surpluses and drug trafficking issues with these countries.

articles.title: Europa cae en la trampa de las tierras raras: China produce, EEUU gestiona y la UE paga
articles.author: Javier Jorrín
articles.summary: La industria europea tiene una alta dependencia de las tierras raras. No sólo es vulnerable a un conflicto con China o con EEUU, también a una escalada entre las dos potencias

articles.title: California’s Grid Being Saved by Batteries, PG&E CEO Says
articles.author: Mark Chediak
articles.summary: California's electrical grid has been maintained during extreme heat thanks to the rapid use of batteries, according to PG&E's CEO. These batteries store energy, allowing the utility to provide power when needed. As a result, residents have not been asked to conserve energy this summer.

articles.title: How to make sense of Donald Trump’s bizarre tariff rates
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Donald Trump extended the tariff truce with China for 90 more days. The U.S. has changed trade rules and tariffs for many countries, including the EU, Japan, and Canada. Some tariffs were reversed after causing problems, like those on gold and Japan’s exports.

articles.title: The economics of the second Trump administration
articles.author: VoxEU | CEPR
articles.summary: President Trump's second term has caused economic problems like higher inflation, growing inequality, and rising living costs for many Americans. His policies, including tariffs and attacks on independent institutions, have created uncertainty that hurts jobs and investment. While the stock market has done well, most benefits go to the wealthy, leaving everyday people feeling frustrated and left behind.

articles.title: Which goods are most vulnerable to American tariffs on China?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: President Trump has imposed an additional 10% tariff on all Chinese goods, escalating the trade war between the U.S. and China. This move will have significant effects on various trade flows, but not all goods will be equally impacted. Charts in the article highlight which products are most vulnerable to these tariffs.

articles.title: El acuerdo comercial UE-Trump: ¿La mejor capitulación posible?
articles.author: J. Jorrín
articles.summary: La Comisión Europea aceptó las condiciones impuestas por Estados Unidos, dilapidando medio siglo de defensa del libre comercio a cambio de evitar un golpe económico mayor

articles.title: One weird trick to solve the affordability crisis
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: Americans have long protested rising food prices, like eggs and meat, causing social unrest. Past government attempts to control prices led to shortages and more inflation. The article suggests a simple, nominal change could help fix the affordability crisis.
articles.reason: Solución simple a crisis de asequibilidad

articles.title: Trump Won’t Stop California’s Clean Power Push, Official Says
articles.author: David R Baker
articles.summary: California is confident it will achieve 100% clean energy despite President Trump's climate policies. The state's economy has grown while reducing carbon emissions, making it the fifth largest economy in the world. California's energy regulator believes the state can succeed in its clean energy goals while still finding common ground with the federal government.

articles.title: Ten indicators explain what’s going on with America’s economy
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: The article discusses ten key indicators that reveal the current state of America's economy under Trumponomics. It uses data and charts to illustrate various economic impacts. Readers can access more detailed analysis by visiting The Economist's Graphic detail page.

articles.title: Narendra Modi plans to free up India’s giant labour force
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: India's labour laws limit the size of factories and the type of workers they hire. This keeps many firms small and relies heavily on contract workers. Prime Minister Narendra Modi plans to remove these restrictions to boost employment and growth.

articles.title: Five Key Charts to Watch in Global Commodity Markets This Week
articles.author: Emma Sanchez
articles.summary: Oil prices are low, around $60 a barrel, leading to a decline in US drilling rigs due to fears of falling demand. Global emissions are expected to drop, but increased fossil fuel use for AI data centers could slow this progress. Meanwhile, China is controlling exports of rare earth metals, which are crucial for technology and defense, while US corn exports are rising due to increased demand.

articles.title: US Solar Firm Enphase to Move Battery Manufacturing Outside of China
articles.author: Mark Chediak
articles.summary: Enphase Energy Inc., a US solar and battery systems supplier, is in the process of moving its battery cell manufacturing out of China to avoid tariffs imposed by President Donald Trump as part of a wider trade war between the world’s two largest economies.

articles.title: US Imposes New Duties on Solar Imports From Southeast Asia
articles.author: Mark Chediak
articles.summary: The US has introduced new tariffs on solar panel imports from Southeast Asia. This move aims to protect domestic manufacturers. The tariffs could increase costs for solar energy projects in the US.

articles.title: Want Solar Panels on Your Roof? How to Navigate Market and Tariff Chaos
articles.author: Todd Woody
articles.summary: Choosing the right solar panel installer is important due to market instability and changing tariffs. In 2024, residential solar installations in the US dropped by 32% compared to the previous year. This highlights the challenges consumers face in the solar market.

articles.title: Visa restrictions are bad for Indians—but maybe not for India
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: Rich countries are making it harder for people to move there for work and study. This means fewer Indians can get visas to go abroad. But this may create new opportunities within India itself.

articles.title: Why Norway’s Political Crisis Is a European Energy Problem
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: The 30-year-long trend of integration and liberalization of the region’s electricity market is at risk.
articles.reason: Crisis política noruega amenaza energía europea

articles.title: The Biggest Losers in the GOP Plan to Slash US Climate Spending
articles.author: Ari Natter
articles.summary: House Republicans proposed a bill to extend Trump's tax cuts by cutting climate spending. This plan targets popular tax credits for electric vehicles and solar energy. The proposed changes aim to dismantle parts of Biden's climate law, which some Republicans criticize as a "green new scam."

articles.title: LA Fires Reveal Limits of California’s $21 Billion Utility Fund
articles.author: Mark Chediak
articles.summary: Los Angeles wildfires are causing significant financial losses, potentially exceeding $30 billion. A $21 billion state fund meant to support utilities may not be enough if companies are held responsible. Concerns are rising, leading to a 20% drop in shares for Edison International, the largest utility in the area.

articles.title: Which are the deadliest European cities in a heatwave?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Heatwaves in Europe cause many deaths, made worse by climate change and poor city design. European cities have higher death rates in extreme heat than similar cities elsewhere, partly because few homes have air conditioning. Without action, heat-related deaths will rise sharply, especially in hot and crowded cities like Florence and Milan.

articles.title: La España sin turistas: diez provincias reciben menos viajeros extranjeros que en 2019
articles.author: Javier Jorrín
articles.summary: Los esfuerzos de las provincias del interior para potenciar los viajes culturales, gastronómicos o naturales no funcionan. Ni siquiera consiguen aprovechar la inflación de los servicios turísticos de las zonas más demandadas

articles.title: The world’s most liveable cities in 2025
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Vienna is no longer the most liveable city in the world. Living standards are at risk due to instability in many places. The article highlights changes in global city rankings for 2025.
articles.reason: Calidad de vida urbana en declive

articles.title: Trump’s first term coincided with a MAGA baby boom. Will his second?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Research shows that after Donald Trump's first election win, more supporters in Republican areas chose to have children compared to those in Democratic areas. This trend resulted in a shift of 1-2% of American births from blue to red counties. As the 2024 election approaches, it's uncertain if a similar baby boom will occur during Trump's potential second term.

articles.title: Un récord esquivo: este año no llegarán a España 100 millones de turistas. Y tampoco en 2026
articles.author: Javier Jorrín
articles.summary: Los datos del verano confirman la ralentización de las llegadas. Todo apunta a que 2025 acabará por debajo de los 98 millones y no se llegará a los 100 millones tampoco en 2026

articles.title: Sunnova’s 71% Stock Plunge Heralds US Solar State of ‘Chaos’
articles.author: Mark Chediak
articles.summary: Sunnova Energy's stock fell 71% as the company expressed doubts about its future. The U.S. solar industry is facing significant challenges due to high interest rates, lower incentives, and policy changes under the Trump administration. Experts warn that uncertainty will continue to impact investments in solar energy throughout the year.

articles.title: Why are girls still falling behind in maths?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Girls are excelling in most school subjects but still struggle with mathematics. This gap can limit their career choices and lower their future earnings. Addressing this issue is important for their long-term success.

articles.title: How will the German election be decided?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: The German election on February 23rd, 2025, may see "none of the above" as a popular choice, as only 40% of voters find Friedrich Merz suitable for chancellor. Economic troubles and immigration debates have revealed significant divisions among the electorate. Five charts illustrate the key trends influencing the election outcome.

articles.title: "Trump entiende mejor el resentimiento contra la élite porque lo ha vivido en persona"
articles.author: Ramón González Férriz
articles.summary: Esteban Hernández, analista político de El Confidencial, publica 'El nuevo espíritu del mundo. Política y geopolítica en la era Trump', un libro que aborda la nueva realidad tras el declive de la globalización
articles.reason: Trump como síntoma del resentimiento anti-élite

articles.title: Economists get cold feet about high minimum wages
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: Economists are worried that very high minimum wages may cause problems. Some governments are trying to raise wages a lot. This could affect jobs and businesses in places like Mexico City.
articles.reason: Preocupación económica por salarios mínimos altos

articles.title: Critical Minerals Are a US Headache, Not an Emergency
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: History tells us engineers and entrepreneurs will deliver the US from the current hysteria.

articles.title: Hard-right parties are now Europe’s most popular
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Hard-right parties have become the most popular political groups in Europe, surpassing traditional conservative and social-democratic parties. In Germany, the Alternative for Germany (AfD) party received over 20% of the vote, its highest since 1933. This rise in support for hard-right parties has occurred despite many being kept out of power.

articles.title: Don’t Fall for the Hype About China’s Critical Minerals Controls
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: The U.S. produces enough molybdenum to meet its needs, despite concerns over China's export controls on critical minerals. The hype around potential shortages is exaggerated, and the U.S. will be fine. Many companies have rebranded to highlight these minerals as "critical" to boost their appeal and share prices.

articles.title: Is an Upside-Down Gas Market Europe's New Normal?
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: A natural gas storage site in Rehden, Germany, is central to Europe's energy crisis as it is nearly empty and needs €2 billion to fill for the winter. The situation raises concerns about heating costs for families and potential inflation. Various players, including traders and regulators, are involved in deciding how to handle the storage issue.

articles.title: What’s wrong with democracy in Europe?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Europe's democracy index improved this year, even as many other regions saw declines. Despite this progress, many European voters feel disappointed and see little to celebrate. The rise of dictators and weak governments elsewhere highlights Europe's unusual stability.

articles.title: Rare Earths in Ukraine? No, Only Scorched Earth.
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: Ukraine does not have significant rare earth mineral deposits, despite claims that it does. The hype around these resources was fueled by misunderstandings and miscalculations, especially from Ukrainians trying to engage with President Trump. In reality, Ukraine's mineral wealth is greatly exaggerated, and the potential value of rare earths is much lower than suggested.

articles.title: Europe’s Gas Refilling Assignment Is Going Well — For Now
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: Europe's gas inventories have risen above 50% of capacity, a welcome improvement from last winter's low levels. However, the region still needs to refill to at least 80% by November to prepare for winter, facing risks from external factors like weather and global supply. Policymakers are adjusting targets and regulations, but there are concerns about entering winter with potentially low gas stockpiles.

articles.title: Tracking Donald Trump’s immigration policy in charts
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Kilmarr Abrego Garcia, a Salvadoran man living legally in the U.S., was unexpectedly deported despite his family's ties to America. He is one of about 200 migrants affected by a recent Trump administration policy that used the Alien Enemies Act. The Supreme Court has ruled that he should be allowed to return, but El Salvador’s president refuses to cooperate.

articles.title: La recaudación del IRPF se dispara un 12% y da margen al Gobierno para negociar el presupuesto
articles.author: Javier Jorrín
articles.summary: España va camino de su primer superávit primario en 18 años. La reducción del déficit es compatible con una aceleración del gasto, gracias a una recaudación disparada

articles.title: Aranceles para reducir déficits: la receta equivocada de Trump
articles.author: C. Dolz
articles.summary: Más allá del repunte de la inflación, que ya afecta a consumidores y a empresas, el déficit comercial crece un 23% tras los adelantos en las importaciones y los ingresos fiscales adicionales por las tasas no podrán aliviar el elevado déficit fiscal
articles.reason: Aranceles fallan en reducir déficit comercial

articles.title: India’s IPO boom is good news for its economy
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: India is experiencing a boom in initial public offerings (IPOs). This growth helps fuel investment and supports economic development. Mumbai's history shows how stockmarket booms can build strong cities and industries.

articles.title: Can the Chinese economy match Aruba’s?
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: Aruba is a small island with only 108,000 people. It doubled its income per person in less than 15 years, even after reaching China’s current income level. China wants to achieve similar economic growth but faces real challenges.
articles.reason: Lecciones de crecimiento desde pequeñas economías

articles.title: Europe Is Better Safe Than Sorry on Natural Gas
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: Replenishing stockpiles, however costly, beats gambling that energy costs will decline next winter.

articles.title: Criminals make up a shrinking share of ICE arrests
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: President Trump increased immigration arrests to deport more criminals. However, fewer people caught are actually criminals. This change may reduce public support for his policies.

articles.title: El INE eleva al 0,8% el crecimiento del PIB del segundo trimestre, el mejor dato en un año
articles.author: Javier Jorrín
articles.summary: Estadística eleva en una décima su primera estimación del PIB. El consumo de los hogares y la inversión explican el crecimiento. En tasa interanual, el crecimiento acelera al 3,1%

articles.title: Six charts show ICE’s expanding immigration crackdown
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Protests against Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) have erupted in cities like Los Angeles, with graffiti expressing anger towards the agency. The demonstrations are a response to President Trump's stricter immigration policies and enforcement actions. Charts in the article illustrate how these policies are affecting immigration enforcement and the challenges that lie ahead.

articles.title: Ice Cream Inflation Arrives Just in Time for Summer
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: Coconut oil prices have skyrocketed, making ice cream more expensive just as summer arrives. This surge is due to a combination of bad weather affecting coconut production and increased demand for biofuels. As a result, consumers may face higher costs for their favorite frozen treats in the near future.

articles.title: Have foreign tourists really avoided America this year?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: America has been in the headlines this year for controversial policies and actions. The Trump administration has cut departments, strained alliances, increased deportations and deployed the National Guard. International tourist numbers have fallen sharply from a year ago.

articles.title: How powerful is your passport?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Not all passports offer the same travel freedom. The American passport is losing its strength compared to others. This means Americans face more travel restrictions than before.

articles.title: Should Anyone Be Buying What Glencore Is Selling?
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: Glencore Plc has an excess of cobalt, a key metal for batteries. To manage this surplus, they are partnering with Cobalt Holdings Plc, which aims to raise $230 million in an IPO. The plan is to buy cobalt from Glencore, store it, and hope prices increase.

articles.title: Schooled by Trump, Americans are learning to dislike their allies
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Polling shows that Americans' views of their allies in Europe and Canada are changing negatively due to President Trump's rhetoric. He has made claims that the European Union is against the U.S. and criticized Canada harshly. This shift in attitude is reshaping how Americans perceive their traditional allies.

articles.title: El Gobierno sube al 2,7% su previsión de crecimiento: "Estamos en un círculo virtuoso"
articles.author: Javier Jorrín
articles.summary: El Gobierno calcula que el PIB sigue creciendo a tasas del 0,7% trimestral pese a las dificultades del contexto internacional. Su previsión es más optimista que la del resto de organismos y del consenso de analistas privados

articles.title: Which countries provide the most, and least, support to Ukraine?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: The U.S. has provided significantly more aid to Ukraine than European countries, according to President Trump. He stated that America has given $350 billion, while Europe has contributed only $100 billion, mostly in loans. This highlights the differences in support levels between the U.S. and Europe for Ukraine.

articles.title: La derecha radical es estructural y legítima. Pero no una oleada imparable
articles.author: Ramón González Férriz
articles.summary: Subió en Portugal, perdió en Rumanía y pasa a la segunda ronda en Polonia. Ya es parte del sistema y se comporta como todos los demás partidos. Aunque sea incómodo, quizá el mejor antídoto sea tratarla como tal

articles.title: Universal child care can harm children
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: Child care in America is very expensive and hard for many families to afford. High costs make some parents have fewer children or stop working. This growing problem worries people because it can harm children and families.

articles.title: Universal child care can hurt children
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: Child care is very expensive for many American families. This cost makes having more children hard and stops some mothers from working. Universal child care might not always help children as expected.

articles.title: Rio Tinto Shareholders Should ‘Trust, But Verify’ Their Board
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: Instead of taking directors at their word, with so much money at stake, investors are within their rights to challenge the status quo.

articles.title: Want Bananas, Coffee, Chocolate? Hello, Import Tax!
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: Trump’s tariffs won’t reverse the US farming deficit, but food inflation will rise and American farmers will suffer.

articles.title: Which countries are most vulnerable to Donald Trump’s aid cuts?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Some countries rely heavily on American aid, with one receiving aid that is 350% of its health spending. Cuts to this aid could significantly impact these nations. The article explores which countries are most vulnerable to these potential reductions.

articles.title: What explains India’s peculiar stability?
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: India remains calm despite unrest in neighboring countries like Nepal, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, and Pakistan. These neighbors face protests and economic crises. India's stability stands out in this challenging region.

articles.title: Trade Wars Won’t Make American Farming Great Again
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: The US trade war is harming food exports and increasing the agricultural trade deficit. American farmers, who once exported more than they imported, are now struggling. The policies of the Trump administration are largely responsible for this decline.

articles.title: UK Financial Regulator Should Fine Itself for LME Fail
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: The 2022 nickel crisis could have cost $20 billion. The FCA still has questions to answer.
articles.reason: Regulador financiero debe multarse a sí mismo

articles.title: WEC Energy Offered $2.5 Billion US Loan for Renewable Projects
articles.author: Ari Natter
articles.summary: WEC Energy received a $2.5 billion loan from the U.S. government. This funding is aimed at supporting renewable energy projects. The loan will help advance cleaner energy initiatives.

articles.title: PG&E Gets $15 Billion Loan Offer From Biden’s Green Bank
articles.author: Ari Natter
articles.summary: PG&E has received a $15 billion loan offer from Biden’s Green Bank. This funding aims to support the company's efforts in improving infrastructure and addressing wildfire risks. The loan reflects the government's commitment to clean energy and safety initiatives.

articles.title: PG&E Gets Record $15 Billion Loan in One of Biden’s Final Acts
articles.author: Mark Chediak
articles.summary: A PG&E Corp. in Calistoga, California.

articles.title: Five charts explain Trump’s cuts to foreign aid
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Donald Trump wants to cut $9.4 billion from government spending, mostly foreign aid. Congress must approve these cuts by July 18th, or the money will be spent as planned. These cuts could save less money than expected and harm many people worldwide.

articles.title: Don’t blame AI for your job woes
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: Tech leaders warn AI will destroy many jobs, but the article argues the real cause of white-collar job loss is the wider economy, not just technology. Economic slowdowns, corporate cost-cutting and weak demand explain the “white-collar chill.” Don’t blame AI alone for current job woes.
articles.reason: IA no culpable de pérdida empleos

articles.title: $50-a-Barrel Oil Is a Problem for US Trade Deficit
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: Shale producers reversed the US oil deficit. Now they face ruin at $50-a-barrel.

articles.title: How do countries rank by military spending?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: The United States has the largest defense budget, exceeding the next nine countries combined. However, comparing military spending requires looking beyond dollar amounts to factors like GDP share and purchasing power. This analysis shows that America's lead may not be as significant as it seems when costs are adjusted for different countries.

articles.title: Illa, el pánico lingüístico y la conversión del PSC en Convergència
articles.author: Ramón González Férriz
articles.summary: Los independentistas creen que, tras el procés, la lengua catalana está herida de muerte. Illa ha respondido con un “pacto nacional” que muestra su objetivo: convertir el socialismo catalán en la nueva Convergència
articles.reason: Conversión del PSC en nuevo convergentismo

articles.title: Investors expect AI use to soar. That’s not happening
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: A recent survey shows that fewer American businesses are using AI at work. Large companies, especially, have reduced their AI adoption. Despite high expectations, demand for AI technology is not growing as fast as predicted.
articles.reason: Adopción empresarial IA menor que expectativas

articles.title: US Oil Output Has Peaked. But Don’t Expect a Rapid Decline.
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: U.S. oil production has likely peaked, but a rapid decline is not expected. Current low prices are causing shale companies to cut spending and reduce drilling activity. The link between oil prices and production means that small price changes can significantly impact output.

articles.title: What Israel and Gaza can learn from past ceasefires
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: The article discusses lessons that Israel and Gaza can learn from past ceasefires. It analyzes data from over 2,000 truces in the last 50 years to assess their effectiveness. The findings could help improve future peace efforts between the two regions.

articles.title: Recessions have become ultra-rare. That is storing up trouble
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: Recessions used to happen very often in the past. Over time, they became less common as economies grew and policies improved. Now, recessions are very rare, but this may cause problems in the future.

articles.title: BP Has One Shot Left in the Last-Chance Saloon
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: BP has prioritized ideology over profits for the past five years, leading to financial struggles and a plummeting market value. The company faces a critical moment at its upcoming capital markets day on February 26, where it must address its high debt while satisfying shareholders. If BP fails to change its approach, it risks an investor revolt and potential job losses among its executives.

articles.title: The UK Is Taxing an Oil Windfall That Doesn’t Exist
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: The UK government has imposed high taxes on the North Sea oil industry, claiming it is benefiting from unusually high prices, but current oil prices are actually low. This has led to declining production and job losses in the sector. The government is now considering a tax review to address these issues and ensure the industry's future viability.

articles.title: How often do ceasefires in the Middle East work?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Donald Trump has announced a ceasefire between Israel and Iran after recent military actions. However, an analysis of over 2,000 truces suggests that this ceasefire may not last. Experts believe Trump's optimism about its longevity is misplaced.

articles.title: Is America’s jobs market nearing a cliff?
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: America’s jobs market is showing signs of weakness despite strong stock market growth. The Federal Reserve has cut interest rates to prevent a deeper downturn. Some leaders want faster rate cuts to support job creation.

articles.title: Why worries about American job losses are overstated
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: Many people fear a big US jobs collapse as growth and job creation diverge. The Fed has cut rates to hedge against a worse downturn. But the article argues the fears are exaggerated.

articles.title: BP Asks Shareholders to Pay for Its Mistakes
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: The oil giant squanders a big chance to win over investors.
articles.reason: BP pide a accionistas pagar errores

articles.title: How do Ukrainian soldier fatalities compare with Russia’s?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Russia is losing more soldiers than Ukraine in the ongoing war. Despite this, the U.S. is pushing for a peace agreement that favors Russia. Ukraine is advised to seek a ceasefire, even though it is not gaining significant territory.

articles.title: La Defensa es tan importante que Sánchez no necesita explicarla
articles.author: Ramón González Férriz
articles.summary: El Gobierno diluyó las nuevas medidas para la protección y la seguridad de España en un debate enmarañado. Es la enésima vez que se hurta a los ciudadanos una explicación detallada sobre una decisión trascendente y cara

articles.title: Shell’s Revamped Strategy Is a ‘Successful Failure’
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: The London-listed oil giant hasn’t succeeded in closing a valuation gap to its US peers. Maybe a change of domicile is needed.

articles.title: The Oil Market Has a Bigger Problem Than a Slowing China – India
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: India's oil demand is not growing fast enough, which is a bigger problem for the oil market than China's slowing economy. While India is seen as a future oil consumption powerhouse, it has not yet reached its potential. As China's growth in oil demand slows, the hope for India's rapid growth remains unfulfilled in 2025.

articles.title: Russia’s summer Ukraine offensive looks like its deadliest yet
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Russia’s summer offensive in Ukraine has caused the highest daily death toll so far, with heavy losses on both sides. Despite intense fighting and more troops, Russia’s land gains remain very small. The war continues with strong Ukrainian resistance and new weapons support from the United States.

articles.title: Oil Market Outlook Gets Lost in (Chinese) Translation
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: Cnooc, CNPC and Sinopec, China’s state-owned oil giants, have boosted the nation’s domestic oil production to a record.

articles.title: Russian sabotage attacks surged across Europe in 2024
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Russian sabotage attacks in Europe have increased sharply since 2022. Germany and Britain warn of rising espionage and hybrid threats from Russia. The recent drop in attacks may be temporary, with more trouble expected soon.

articles.title: Montero se desmarca de la propuesta de financiación autonómica que está ultimando Illa
articles.author: Javier Jorrín
articles.summary: La vicepresidenta toma distancias con la propuesta que está preparando la Generalitat y ha puesto al ministerio a trabajar en un texto alternativo sin fecha de publicación

articles.title: How Donald Trump can dodge a Supreme Court tariff block
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: Donald Trump called the trade deficit a national emergency. The U.S. has had trade deficits since the 1970s. Even if the Supreme Court blocks tariffs, the president has other powers to act.
articles.reason: Trump puede esquivar bloqueo judicial aranceles

articles.title: US Enjoys a Rare Moment of Oil Supremacy in Iran
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: The US is now the world's top oil producer and imports very little oil from the Middle East. This reduces America's dependence on unstable regions like Iran and Saudi Arabia. However, global oil prices still rise if conflicts disrupt supply, showing the US is not fully free from risks.

articles.title: Maps and data tell the story of two bloody years in Israel and Gaza
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Two years of war between Israel and Hamas have caused massive death and destruction in Gaza, with thousands killed and much of the region in ruins. The conflict has displaced many Palestinians, caused severe hunger, and deepened tensions throughout the Middle East. Even if a ceasefire is reached, lasting peace and rebuilding remain uncertain.

articles.title: Hacienda tiene lista la nueva financiación autonómica y espera la luz verde de Sánchez
articles.author: Marcos Lamelas. Barcelona
articles.summary: Moncloa ha decidido activar la reforma del sistema de financiación que lleva años paralizada. La Generalitat de Cataluña ya conoce la propuesta del Gobierno que servirá para todas las comunidades del Régimen Común

articles.title: The new globalisation paradox
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: Brazil’s presidential palace symbolizes national strength but faces internal and external pressures. In 2023, supporters of former president Bolsonaro attacked the palace, showing political unrest. Also, President Trump imposed high tariffs on Brazilian goods, revealing how global powers influence trade and politics.

articles.title: Satellite images show how receiving aid in Gaza became so deadly
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Hundreds of Palestinians have died trying to get food aid in Gaza, mostly from gunfire and stampedes near distribution sites. The Gaza Humanitarian Foundation runs four main aid hubs inside dangerous zones, making access hard and chaotic. International groups have criticized Israel for limiting aid, and recent pauses in fighting aim to bring in more supplies.

articles.title: The mystery of America’s shutdown economy
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: The US government shutdown has stopped important economic data from being released. Different unofficial sources give conflicting information about the economy. This makes it hard to understand how America’s economy is really doing.

articles.title: The trouble with America’s shutdown economy
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: America’s government shutdown is blocking key economic data from the Bureau of Labor Statistics. Without official figures, analysts must rely on conflicting unofficial reports. The uncertainty makes it harder to judge the true state of the economy.

articles.title: The problem with America’s shutdown economy
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: Gridlock in Washington prevents official data releases. And unofficial ones disagree

articles.title: How much of Gaza is left standing?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Much of Gaza is destroyed, with around 70% of buildings damaged and many people killed. The population is crowded into a small area with few services working, causing many deaths beyond the fighting. Rebuilding will cost billions, and the full impact is only clear after the war ends.

articles.title: Radiografía al Gobierno: Sánchez intenta tapar la parálisis legislativa con propaganda ministerial
articles.author: Borja Negrete
articles.summary: El Ejecutivo, PSOE y Sumar tienen un centenar de iniciativas pendientes de tramitación en el Congreso y algunas ya acumulan más de 50 prórrogas y 18 meses de retraso. Los 22 departamentos trabajan para que Moncloa venda que hay proyecto

articles.title: How AI is breaking cover letters
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: AI is changing how cover letters are written and read. This makes it harder for employers to find the best candidates. As a result, workers may end up earning lower pay.

articles.title: California Vows to Defend Car Emissions Rules Despite Trump
articles.author: Mark Chediak
articles.summary: California plans to defend its vehicle emissions rules against President Trump's efforts to revoke them. Liane Randolph, the chair of the California Air Resources Board, emphasized the state's commitment to clean air and will actively fight for its regulations. Meanwhile, concerns about wildfire impacts are affecting investor confidence in California’s utilities.

articles.title: The evolution of famine in Gaza, in maps and charts
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Parts of Gaza are now facing a severe famine, with over half a million people suffering from extreme hunger. The crisis is worst in Gaza city and is expected to spread to other areas soon. More than 270 people, including many children, have died from starvation, though some officials dispute these numbers.

articles.title: The Black Market for Oil Will Continue to Thrive
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: Unless Western governments are ready to accept higher prices, Russia, Venezuela and Iran will continue to evade sanctions.

articles.title: America’s huge bunker-busting bomb is not sure to work in Iran
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Israel has attacked some of Iran's nuclear facilities, but many are still protected underground. The U.S. has a powerful bomb called the Massive Ordnance Penetrator that could potentially reach these deep sites. President Trump has hinted at supporting action against Iran, saying patience is running out.

articles.title: The mystery of China’s slumping investment
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: China’s investment in infrastructure, manufacturing and construction has fallen sharply. The drops are so large some analysts doubt the data. Officials do not seem very worried.

articles.title: The Return of Russian Commodities? They Never Really Left
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: Only natural gas could see a big drop in prices once trade normalizes.

articles.title: Mapping Iran’s nuclear programme
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: After recent American strikes on Iran, officials are unsure how much damage was done to its nuclear program. Some facilities may have survived, and Iran might have moved some of its highly enriched uranium to secret locations. While producing a bomb remains difficult, concerns persist about Iran's potential to resume its nuclear activities.

articles.title: Gastar mucho e invertir poco: el (otro) gran error de Sánchez
articles.author: Ramón González Férriz
articles.summary: Bonos para la cultura, el transporte o el alquiler. Subidas de pensiones y sueldos de funcionarios. Sánchez lo ha fiado todo a aumentar el gasto público, pero ha sacrificado la inversión a largo plazo por interés electoral

articles.title: China’s property market is (somehow) worsening
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: China's property market is getting worse despite government efforts to fix it. The country's top leaders tried to stabilize the market in 2024 but failed. The situation remains unstable and troubling.

articles.title: Iran has come back online—for now
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Iran has paused missile attacks after a fragile ceasefire with Israel. The war allowed new generals to gain power, reducing the clerics' control. The regime has relaxed some internet restrictions but remains tense about the future.

articles.title: Investors are telling Britain to cheer up a bit
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: Britain has real economic problems, but its assets are doing better than expected. Investors are encouraging the country to be more optimistic. Despite challenges, there are reasons to feel hopeful about the future.

articles.title: A short history of the Koreas, in eight maps
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Korea was once one nation called hanminjok. Now, it is divided into North Korea and South Korea by barbed wire and landmines. The article uses eight maps to show Korea’s history from the past to today.

articles.title: A short history of Greenland, in six maps
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: The article discusses Greenland's historical significance and its strategic value due to its resources and geography. Donald Trump has expressed interest in acquiring Greenland, which has raised concerns among America's allies. Climate change has made the island more important by opening new sea routes.

articles.title: Swapping Oil Barrels for Pork Barrels Is a Terrible Trade
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: The U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) is running low, currently holding about 400 million barrels, down from a high of 727 million in 2010. Politicians have misused the reserve for unrelated public spending, and it is projected to shrink further in the coming years. A strong emergency stockpile is crucial for responding to global oil market shocks, but lawmakers seem unwilling to invest in it.

articles.title: A visual guide to critical materials and rare earths
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: President Trump is interested in acquiring Greenland mainly for its rare earth materials. These materials are important for modern economies and military operations. A U.S. delegation is visiting Greenland to learn more about its culture and resources.

articles.title: Our Fed tracker: how much will its independence be compromised?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Donald Trump is trying to influence the Federal Reserve by firing some governors and appointing others who favor lower interest rates. Betting markets show most people think current leaders will stay until at least the end of 2025. Investors are watching closely but have not reacted strongly to these political moves yet.

articles.title: Investors will help Jamaica recover from Hurricane Melissa
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: Jamaica was hit by Hurricane Melissa, its worst storm ever. The country will receive $150 million from catastrophe bonds issued in 2024. This payout will help Jamaica recover from the disaster.

articles.title: Sunnova Energy Plunges After Issuing Going Concern Warning
articles.author: Mark Chediak
articles.summary: Sunnova Energy's stock fell 50% after warning it may not survive due to cash flow problems. High interest rates and reduced state incentives have made solar equipment more expensive for consumers. The company's market value has dropped significantly, and forecasts for solar installations are declining.

articles.title: The factions jostling for Donald Trump’s favour
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: The Republican Party is united by strong loyalty to Donald Trump. However, different groups within the party disagree on issues like taxes and trade. Trump has managed to keep these groups together, but his successor may find this difficult.

articles.title: El extraño regreso de la religión a la política
articles.author: Ramón González Férriz
articles.summary: La religiosidad cae. La sociedad es más laica y ha dejado de obedecer las enseñanzas de la iglesia. Pero los líderes y los medios están volviendo a dar a la religión un papel preponderante en la política

articles.title: Oil Is Cheap But It Isn’t (Yet) a Bargain
articles.author: Javier Blas
articles.summary: Oil prices have dropped significantly, making them seem cheap, but they might not be a bargain yet. Current market conditions suggest prices could fall further, potentially below $50 a barrel, before stabilizing. However, history shows that oil tends to recover quickly after downturns, so investing in long-term oil futures could be a smart move for those willing to take risks.

articles.title: Ten charts to explain Trump’s big, beautiful bill
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: The U.S. Senate passed a major bill called the One Big Beautiful Bill Act after long debate. The House must agree with the Senate before President Trump can sign it into law. This bill will change taxes and spending and may cause problems for America.

articles.title: Which Kevin Hassett would lead the Federal Reserve?
articles.author: Finance & economics
articles.summary: Kevin Hassett is a well-known economist with experience at the Federal Reserve. President Trump may choose him as the Fed’s next chair, which would change how the Fed and White House work together. This decision has a high chance of happening, according to prediction markets.

articles.title: Peabody Reviews $3.8 Billion Coal Deal After Anglo Mine Fire
articles.author: Mark Chediak
articles.summary: Peabody Energy is reviewing a $3.8 billion deal to purchase Anglo American's coal business after an incident at an Australian mine led to worker evacuations. The company is considering its options while still negotiating with Anglo. This deal aims to shift Peabody's focus more towards metallurgical coal.

articles.title: Which stockmarkets have benefited from the chaos on Wall Street?
articles.author: Graphic detail
articles.summary: Since Donald Trump's inauguration, the S&P 500 has dropped over 9%, and the Nasdaq has fallen nearly 15%. Investors are moving away from tech giants, which have lost a quarter of their value, and are seeking safer investments like gold or stocks from other countries. This shift reflects a focus on stability rather than high returns.
